name: scaffolding notes
top_issues: C9 — Standards alignment is absent where it's clearly applicable. A lesson design article for K-12 science and ELA contexts should name at least one relevant framework (e.g., CCSS ELA literacy standards for note-taking such as CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RST.6-8.1, or NGSS science practices like 'Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information'). The article cites research authors but never anchors the strategies to actual standards teachers are accountable to.

C10 — Wayground's value proposition is entirely generic. Phrases like 'standards-aligned resources with built-in accommodations' and 'Wayground helps you meet every student where they are' appear three times but never name a specific feature. A teacher reading this cannot picture what they would actually click on in Wayground. Replace at least one mention with something concrete — e.g., 'Wayground's Create with AI lets you generate a three-tier scaffolded note template in under 5 minutes by inputting your lesson objective and target grade.'

C5 — Grade-band specificity is thin outside of 7th-grade science. The article leans heavily on one scenario (7th-grade science, chemical reactions, photosynthesis) but the title and meta description promise K-12 breadth. Add at least one explicit example from a different grade band and subject — e.g., a 3rd-grade ELA reading lesson or a 10th-grade history class — to match the scope claimed.
positive_signals: The three-tier differentiation table (High/Moderate/Low support) is genuinely useful and photocopy-ready — exactly the kind of visual reference a teacher would bookmark during planning.
Cognitive Load Theory and the gradual release of responsibility framework are both defined inline and cited, which respects novice teachers without talking down to experienced ones.
narrative: This article has real bones — the differentiation table is classroom-ready, the research citations are handled cleanly, and teacher agency is consistently respected throughout. However, it cannot pass in its current state because two criteria hit automatic-revise thresholds: Wayground is mentioned four times without ever naming a single specific feature (a teacher still has no idea what to click), and a lesson-design article aimed at K-12 teachers never once references a standards framework teachers are actually evaluated against, such as CCSS literacy standards or NGSS science practices. Fix those two issues first, then broaden the grade-band examples beyond the single 7th-grade science scenario to match the K-12 scope the title and meta description promise.
